preview_rows.claim2_abstract: Resultssuggest that residing in a single-mother familywas associated with small increases in behaviorproblems and decreases in achievement and thatresiding in a social-father family was associatedwith small increases in behavior problems.
preview_rows.claim3_hyp: In addition, stableresidence in both single-mother and social-fatherfamilies was associated with increased behaviorproblems over time, and these effects weresimilar to those for children experiencing familytransitions (0.15 and 0.18 SD, respectively)
preview_rows.claim3b_testspec: longitudinal data from the Maternal andChild Supplement of the National LongitudinalSurvey of Youth (N = 3,862) | Behaviorproblems are assessed by the total score onthe Behavioral Problems Index (BPI; Zill& Peterson, 1986). Mothers are asked 28items about their child’s behavior. Responseswere dichotomized to indicate the presence orabsence of problem behaviors and then summed;resulting scores range from 0 to 28. | We used observations of children at (on average)ages 6, 8, 10, and 12 to model academicachievement and behavioral trajectories duringmiddle childhood with hierarchical linearmodels (HLM; Bryk & Raudenbush, 1992).Children’s initial levels of achievement andbehavior problems at age 6 were modeled asa function of their prior and current familystructures. Changes in children’s outcomes weremodeled as a function of family structure statesand transitions between ages 6 and 12. | The initial level (P0i ) and subsequent linearslope (P1i ) were taken as outcomes and predictedby family structure states and transitions, timeinvariant family and child controls, and randomerror terms. | The keyparameter of interest in these models is Bt 1 ,which indicates whether children experiencinga particular family structure state or transitionbetween ages 6 and 12 exhibited different ratesof change in their behavior and achievementtrajectories than those residing with both of theirbiological parents throughout this time period.
preview_rows.coded_claim2: Results suggest that residing in a single-mother family was associated with small increases in behavior problems...
preview_rows.coded_claim3: stable residence in a single-mother family is associated with increased behavior problems over time.
preview_rows.coded_claim3b: The claim was tested using longitudinal data from the Maternal and Child Supplement of the National Longitudinal Survey of Youth (N = 3,862). The authors used observations of children at (on average) ages 6, 8, 10, and 12 to model academic achievement and behavioral trajectories during middle childhood with hierarchical linear models. Children’s initial levels of achievement and behavior problems at age 6 were modeled as a function of their prior and current family structures. Changes in children’s outcomes were modeled as a function of family structure states and transitions between ages 6 and 12. The initial level and subsequent linear slope were taken as outcomes and predicted by family structure states and transitions, time invariant family and child controls, and random error terms. The key parameter of interest in these models indicates whether children experiencing a particular family structure state or transition between ages 6 and 12 exhibited different rates of change in their behavior trajectories than those residing with both of their biological parents throughout this time period (the reference category in the statistical models). The focal comparison was between the reference category (always residing in a two, biological parent family)  and a dummy variable indicating stable resident in a single-mother family. Controls for child age, race or ethnicity, gender, and mother’s academic aptitude (AFQT score) are used to predict children’s achievement and behavior slopes. The dependent variable , behavior problems was assessed by the total score on the Behavioral Problems Index (BPI; Zill& Peterson, 1986). Mothers are asked 28 items about their child’s behavior. Responses were dichotomized to indicate the presence or absence of problem behaviors and then summed; resulting scores range from 0 to 28.
preview_rows.coded_claim4: The result was a statistically significant coefficient for stable residence in a single-mother family (reference category was always residing in a two, biological parent family) - standardized beta = .16, SE = .05, p < .01.
preview_rows.coded_sample_size: 3,862 subjects over 4 waves of data collection (panel data)

preview_rows.claim2_abstract: We find that students with able roommates perform better, and the magnitude of  this  roommate  effect  increases  when  the  roommate’s  skills  match  the  student’s  academic goals.
preview_rows.claim3_hyp: Hypothesis  2:  The  academic  achievement  of  a student’s peers will have a greater effect on the  student’s  academic  performance  when there  is  a  match  between  peers’  resources and the focal student’s goals. | Our  results  do  indicate  a  relationship  between the  skills  of  a  focal  student’s  roommates  and the  focal  student’s  subsequent  performance  in specific  fields  of  study.  Focal  students  who have  mathematically  skilled  roommates  do better in mathematics and science but not Eng-lish.
preview_rows.claim3b_testspec: A key strength of our study is that roommates were  randomly  assigned.  However,  we  also include  a  substantial  set  of  control  variables in our analysis to ensure the exogeneity of our main  independent  variables  (Imai,  King,  and Stuart  2008).  Our  estimations  include  a  vari-able  indicating  whether  a  student  is  male  or female;  approximately  44.3  percent  of  stu-dents  are  female.  Our  dataset  also  includes information about students’ caste category. In our  models,  the  caste  variable  indicates  stu-dents’  actual  caste  category  and  not  the  cate-gory  through  which  students  were  admitted. That  is,  a  BC-A  student  admitted  through  an open  category  seat  is  classified  as  BC-A,  not OC.  Approximately  63  percent  of  students attended  local  government  high  schools, which often have fewer resources than private schools. We  include  an  indicator  variable  for government school to capture any differences in student performance arising from this fact.Finally,  students  enrolled  at  this  university come  from  23  districts  across  the  state, although 88 percent of students hail from just nine districts. Our models include fixed effects for  each  district.  Finally,  we  cluster  standard errors  at  the  dormitory-room  level  to  account for multiple observations within a room. Table 1 summarizes the relevant variables. | Are Social Capital Effects Stronger within Disciplinary Subjects? Second-Semester Academic Performance in Chemistry/Physics, Mathematics, and English, Regressed on Roommates’ Pretreatment Academic Performance in Mathematics and Social Science | As previously mentioned, we  computed  students  pre-  and  post-treatment  aggregate  scores  from  specific  subjects. Regarding  pretreatment  scores,  we  have  data on how students performed in mathematics and social  science.  With  respect  to  post-treatment scores,  we  have  disaggregated  data  on  how students performed in physics/chemistry, math-ematics,  and  English.  We  should  note  that within-student  scores  in  various  subjects  are correlated. Students who did well in mathemat-ics also did well in the social sciences. However, a  student’s  performance  across  subjects  is  not perfectly correlated. We thus ask whether hav-ing  roommates  skilled  in  social  science,  for instance,  helps  a  focal  student  improve  her score in mathematics. Similarly, we ask whether roommates  skilled  in  mathematics  (because they have good study habits) will affect a focal student’s performance in English. Table 3 pro-vides  some  evidence  that  roommate  effects depend  on  whether  a  match  exists  between roommates’  skills  and  the  subject  a  focal  stu-dent  is  studying.  The  table  presents  estimates that  examine  how  focal  students  perform  in chemistry/physics,  mathematics,  and  English as a function of their roommates’ performance
preview_rows.coded_claim2: We find that students with able roommates perform better, and the magnitude of  this  roommate  effect  increases  when  the  roommate’s  skills  match  the  student’s  academic goals.
preview_rows.coded_claim3: There is a relationship between the skills of a focal student’s roommates and the focal student’s subsequent performance in specific fields of study: focal students who have mathematically skilled roommates do better in mathematics
preview_rows.coded_claim3b: Students’ semester 2 math scores were regressed on their own and their roommate’s math and social science scores at entry, as well as control variables including gender, caste category and school type, with fixed effects for district and clustered standard errors at the dormitory-room level to account for multiple observations within a room.
preview_rows.coded_claim4: The pre-treatment math score of the roommate has a significant impact on the math scores of the focal student at semester 2, coefficient = 0.074, p < 0.01, two-tailed.

preview_rows.claim2_abstract: Finally, we ﬁndthat controllable and uncontrollable processes are both predictive of product choiceand consumption
preview_rows.claim3_hyp: This study’s main goal was to investigate whether theuncontrollable EC effect on attitudes documented in theprevious studies influences actual product choices and con-sumption. | To study the effects on product choices, weagain used the initially neutral logos of bottled waterbrands as our CS repertory, which would be paired withpositive versus negative pictures during the conditioningphase. We then assessed the choices of participants whenthey were given the opportunity to taste these waters
preview_rows.claim3b_testspec: We conducted three HLMs to assess the (stan-dardized) c- and u-parameters’ impact on the attitudinalEC effect (i.e., the difference in attitudes between posi-tively and negatively conditioned brands), the brand choiceEC effect (i.e., the difference in cups chosen of these
preview_rows.claim4_inftest: Again, the u-parameter had a universally positive influence (B ¼ 0.62,SE ¼ 0.11, t(71) ¼ 5.41, p < .001)
preview_rows.coded_claim2: [W]e find that…uncontrollable processes are… predictive of product choice.
preview_rows.coded_claim3: Study 5 asks whether there is a relationship between the uncontrollable EC effect in the multinomial processing tree model fit to data from participants in the EC procedure, and the actual product choices of those participants.
preview_rows.coded_claim3b: The analysis conducted an HLM to asses the standardized u-parameter’s impact on the brand choice EC effect.
preview_rows.coded_claim4: In an HLM model fit to the data from study 5, the u (uncontrollable choice) -parameter had a universally positive influence on the evaluative conditioning (EC) effect of brand choice (B = 0.62, SE = 0.11, t(71) = 5.41, p < .001)
preview_rows.sample_size: Eighty-one students | MPT modelingwas based on 1,944 observations

preview_rows.claim2_abstract: If help is chosen on around-by-round basis, the probability of escaping the performance trap is slightly reduced by allowing for help.
preview_rows.claim3_hyp: Minimum effort will be higher for theForced Commitment treatment than for either the No Help | treatment
preview_rows.claim4_inftest: Compared with the NoHelp treatment, the Forced Commitment treatment makeslow-ability types better off without harming the high-abilitytypes.
preview_rows.coded_claim2: If help is chosen on around-by-round basis, the probability of escaping the performance trap is slightly reduced by allowing for help.
preview_rows.coded_claim3: Minimum effort will be higher for the Forced Commitment treatment than for the No Help treatment.
preview_rows.coded_claim4: Compared with the No Help treatment, the Forced Commitment treatment make slow-ability types better off without harming the high-ability types.

preview_rows.claim2_abstract: Across time, space, and subsectors, wefind that financial deregulation is associated with skill intensity, job complexity,and high wages for finance employees.
preview_rows.claim3_hyp: the time-series regressionsconfirm the strong link between deregulation, skill upgrading,and wages in finance.

We fit regressions ofthe type | ð9Þ | Theresults for regressions in levels are reported in Table III, PanelB.

preview_rows.coded_claim3b: The paper fits simple predictive regressions of relative wages. The regressions ask the following question: If financial regulation tightens for five years, what should one predict about future relative wages? Regressions in levels give more weight to regulation, as one can expect from examining Figure VIIIC. The paper fits regressions of the type [equation] (9). The results for regressions in levels are reported in Table III, PanelB, last column. The focal independent variable is “Deregulation Index, t-5”.
preview_rows.coded_claim4: The results for regressions in levels are reported in Table III, PanelB. In these regressions the effect of deregulation is relatively stable across specifications and plays a significant role. [coefficient for “Deregulation Index, t-5” is 0.0877, p < 0.01]
